# pr-minder

> pr-minder is a GitHub App (running as a Cloudflare Worker) that keeps pull requests healthy with zero per-repo workflow files. It keeps PR branches up to date with their base, syncs labels with GitHub's native auto-merge, flags PRs that have merge conflicts with a label, revives "zombie" PRs whose CI never ran, opens PRs for forgotten branches, and can write PR titles and descriptions from the diff with an AI model. It is opt-in and event-driven, with a lightweight periodic sweep that re-checks only the PRs it has explicitly flagged — deferred zombie re-checks and pending merge-conflict re-checks — plus an auto-merge backstop (no work, and no GitHub calls, when nothing is pending). It keeps a little dedup state (which PRs it has already checked) in Workers KV.

## Install

Install the GitHub App here: https://github.com/apps/pr-minder/installations/new

Pick "All repositories" or a selected set. pr-minder does nothing until you add a config file, so installing it is safe — every behavior is opt-in.

## How it works

pr-minder receives GitHub webhooks (`pull_request`, `pull_request_review`, `push`, `installation`, `installation_repositories`). For each event it verifies the signature, mints a short-lived installation token, loads your config, and takes the configured actions through the GitHub REST and GraphQL APIs. There is no stored state.

Because it acts with the App's own credentials (not a workflow's default `GITHUB_TOKEN`), the PRs it creates and reopens trigger their workflows normally — which is the basis of the zombie-PR features below.

## Configuration

Config files are JSONC — `//` line comments and `/* */` block comments are allowed.

pr-minder resolves config in this order (first match wins):

1. `<repo>/.github/config/pr-minder/pr-minder.jsonc` — per-repo config (highest priority).
2. `<org>/.github` repo, at `.github/config/pr-minder/pr-minder.jsonc` (the same path) — org-wide defaults. Per-repo overrides go under `repos.<repo-name>`; top-level keys are the org default.
3. No config found — everything is disabled.

Config is cached for up to 60 seconds, so an edit can take about a minute to take effect.

Add the schema for editor validation and autocomplete:

```jsonc
{
  "$schema": "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/wow-look-at-my/pr-minder/master/schema/pr-minder.schema.json"
}
```

There is no global "enabled" switch. A feature is on only when its section is present and configured; "disable" means removing the section (or setting `auto_open_pr.enabled` / `auto_trigger_workflows` to `false`, or leaving `auto_update_pr.triggers` empty).

### auto_update_pr — keep PR branches up to date

`auto_update_pr.triggers` is a list of condition objects. When **any** condition is satisfied, pr-minder updates the PR branch with its base (GitHub's "Update branch"). Keys **within** one condition are ANDed; multiple conditions in the array are ORed. Updating is re-evaluated on PR events, on approving reviews, and on every push to the default branch.

| Key | Meaning |
|-----|---------|
| `label` | PR has this label. |
| `approved_by` | PR is approved by at least one of these usernames. |
| `min_approvals` | PR has at least this many approvals (from anyone). |

```jsonc
{
  "auto_update_pr": {
    "triggers": [
      { "label": "automerge" },
      { "approved_by": ["alice", "bob"], "min_approvals": 2 }
    ]
  }
}
```

### auto_label_pr — labels, auto-update, and auto-merge

A map of label name to per-label settings:

| Field | Meaning |
|-------|---------|
| `auto_add` | `"on_pr_creation"` applies the label when a PR is opened; `false` (default) disables. |
| `create_label_if_missing_in_repo` | `true` creates the label in the repo (using `color`) if it doesn't exist. |
| `color` | 6-digit hex, with or without a leading `#` (default `00FF00`). |
| `mode` | `"auto_update"`: keep the PR branch in sync with base whenever this label is present. `"auto_merge"`: bidirectionally sync this label with GitHub's native auto-merge. `"merge_conflict"`: pr-minder adds this label when the PR has a merge conflict and removes it when the conflict is resolved (see below). |
| `auto_merge_method` | `"merge"`, `"squash"` (default), or `"rebase"`. Only used with `mode: "auto_merge"`. |

With `mode: "auto_merge"`, adding the label arms GitHub's native auto-merge and removing it disarms it; toggling auto-merge from the GitHub UI adds/removes the label to match. Whenever GitHub won't arm native auto-merge, pr-minder merges the PR directly using `auto_merge_method` — this covers both an already-mergeable PR (GitHub refuses auto-merge on a "clean" PR) and a repo that hasn't enabled "Allow auto-merge" in its settings, so the label still merges your PRs there. Branch protection still gates every merge, and a PR that isn't mergeable yet is left untouched. (For the "wait for checks, then merge" behavior in a repo without native auto-merge, enable "Allow auto-merge" in the repo's Settings; pr-minder uses native auto-merge whenever the repo allows it.)

Day to day this is driven by the `labeled` webhook, so it acts the moment you add the label. As a safety net (not a poll), pr-minder also runs a backstop that **searches your whole installation for labeled-but-unarmed PRs** and arms (or merges) them: on startup after each redeploy, every few minutes on a schedule, and shortly after each webhook. Because it searches by label, its cost scales with the number of labeled PRs, not the number of repos — so it stays cheap across large installations. A PR labeled while an older version was running, or one whose webhook was somehow missed, gets healed automatically within a few minutes.

```jsonc
{
  "auto_label_pr": {
    "automerge": { "mode": "auto_merge", "auto_merge_method": "squash", "create_label_if_missing_in_repo": true, "color": "0e8a16" },
    "auto-update": { "mode": "auto_update", "auto_add": "on_pr_creation", "create_label_if_missing_in_repo": true }
  }
}
```

### merge_conflict label — flag PRs that have conflicts

A label with `mode: "merge_conflict"` is managed entirely by pr-minder: it **adds the label when a PR has a merge conflict** with its base and **removes it once the conflict is resolved**. Don't add or remove the label by hand. Opt-in — there is no such label unless you configure one.

```jsonc
{
  "auto_label_pr": {
    "merge-conflict": { "mode": "merge_conflict", "create_label_if_missing_in_repo": true, "color": "d73a4a" }
  }
}
```

A PR can become conflicted when its own head changes or when its base advances, and both are covered: the PR's `opened` / `reopened` / `synchronize` events re-check it immediately, and a push to the default branch flags every open PR for a re-check (any of them may now conflict). On install — and the first time pr-minder sees an already-installed repo — it flags the existing open PRs too.

GitHub computes mergeability **asynchronously** and emits no webhook with the result, so right after a base/head move it briefly reports "unknown". pr-minder handles this by flagging the PR and settling the label from the periodic sweep once the answer is ready (usually within a few minutes) — so the label can lag a base change slightly but always converges. Mergeability reflects **conflicts only** (not failing checks or missing reviews), so the label means exactly "this needs a rebase/merge". Draft PRs are not labeled.

### auto_trigger_workflows — revive "zombie" PRs

`auto_trigger_workflows` is a boolean (default `false`).

A PR created with a workflow's default `GITHUB_TOKEN` (author `github-actions[bot]`) never triggers its own workflows — GitHub suppresses those runs to prevent recursion — so it sits open with no CI and can never go green. pr-minder revives these by closing and reopening the PR with its own App credentials, which fires a fresh event that runs the workflows for real. It only ever acts on PRs authored by `github-actions[bot]` (the kind that can be a `GITHUB_TOKEN` zombie).

It checks each PR **at most once per commit**, remembering what it has seen in Workers KV (keyed by the PR's head commit): a PR is only evaluated when its current commit hasn't been checked before, so an untouched PR is never re-checked, while new commits (a "touched" PR) are. When does it check?

- **Live events** — a PR being opened, reopened, or pushed to (`synchronize`) is checked immediately.
- **First time it sees a repo** — on the first webhook of any kind from a repo, it checks that repo's existing open PRs once. This is what revives zombies in repos that were *already* installed before the feature existed (GitHub never re-sends their install event), with no reinstall needed — the repo's next bit of activity heals it.
- **On install / adding repos** — it checks the open PRs in the repos being added.
- **A short safety sweep (every few minutes)** — a brand-new commit that has no workflow runs *yet* is ambiguous (its CI may simply not have registered), so pr-minder waits about a minute before deciding. It flags only that specific PR and a periodic sweep re-checks it once it has aged: if CI showed up it does nothing; if the commit is a genuine zombie it revives it. The sweep does no work and makes no GitHub calls when nothing is flagged. This is also what keeps pr-minder's own branch-update merge from being treated as a zombie.

It will not loop: pr-minder's own reopen comes back as a `reopened` event sent by a bot, which is skipped.

```jsonc
{ "auto_trigger_workflows": true }
```

### auto_open_pr — open PRs for forgotten branches

`auto_open_pr` opens a PR for any branch that is ahead of its base and has no open PR, **using pr-minder's App credentials** so the PR triggers its workflows normally (it is never a zombie). This is the root-cause fix for the "a CI job opened a PR with `GITHUB_TOKEN` and CI never ran" problem.

| Field | Meaning |
|-------|---------|
| `enabled` | Turn the feature on (default `false`). |
| `skip_branches` | Extra branch names (exact match) to never open PRs for. The default branch and `gh-pages` are always skipped. |
| `skip_branch_patterns` | Extra branches to never open PRs for, as regex patterns matched against the branch name (anchor with `^...$`). Excludes a whole family at once — e.g. `["^\\d+\\.\\d+\\.\\d+$"]` keeps version branches like `2.1.81` from each becoming a PR head. A malformed pattern simply never matches. |
| `target_base` | Base branch for opened PRs (default: the repo's default branch). When `base_from_fork_point` is on, this is the fallback used when no fork point is detected. |
| `base_from_fork_point` | Detect each branch's base from its **fork point** instead of always using `target_base` (default `false`). |
| `base_branch_patterns` | When `base_from_fork_point` is on, which non-default branches may be selected as a base (regex patterns). The default branch always qualifies. |
| `close_when_empty` | Close **any** open PR with a zero net diff against the base, regardless of who opened it (default `true`). See below. |
| `delete_branch_when_empty` | When `close_when_empty` closes a content-empty PR, also delete its head branch (default `false`). See below. |

It runs on a push to a non-default branch (opening the PR as soon as the branch gets a commit) and sweeps existing branches once when the App is installed or repositories are added. Fork branches are not opened.

**Closing content-empty PRs (`close_when_empty`, default on).** A 0-diff PR — no net change against its base — has nothing to review or merge and only wastes CI on each update, so pr-minder **closes** it. It doesn't matter how the PR became empty (a squash-merge orphan that's ahead by commit count but has no net content, or a PR opened with real changes whose content later landed in the base another way) or **who opened it** — a human's empty PR is closed too (closing is reversible: it can be reopened). For each open non-draft PR it closes — with a one-line comment — only when the net diff against the base is exactly zero (an unknown/omitted file count is left alone). This runs when the default branch advances (a push to it) and on the install/backfill sweeps. Set `close_when_empty: false` to opt out.

**Deleting the branch too (`delete_branch_when_empty`, default off).** When `close_when_empty` closes a content-empty PR, pr-minder can also delete that PR's head branch, so closed branches don't pile up. The branch is content-identical to its base (zero net diff), so deleting it loses nothing. Only a branch **in this repository** is deleted — a fork PR's head branch (which lives in the fork, and which pr-minder can't write to) is left untouched — and GitHub itself refuses to delete the default or a protected branch. It has no effect unless `close_when_empty` is also on, and is off by default because deleting a branch is the one part of the empty-PR cleanup that isn't trivially reversible. Set `delete_branch_when_empty: true` to enable.

**Fork-point base detection.** With `base_from_fork_point: true`, pr-minder picks the base by walking the branch's commits newest-first and taking the first ancestor that is the HEAD of another *qualifying* branch — the default branch, or one matching `base_branch_patterns`. This suits an archive/version-branch layout: an investigation branch forked from a long-lived version branch (whose tip never moves, so it *is* the fork point) is opened back into that version branch, while a branch forked off the default branch goes into the default branch. When no qualifying fork point is found it falls back to `target_base` (or the default branch).

```jsonc
{
  "auto_open_pr": {
    "enabled": true,
    "skip_branches": ["staging"],
    "target_base": ""
  }
}
```

Archive-repo example — open each working branch back into the version branch it forked from, and never open a PR from a version branch itself:

```jsonc
{
  "auto_open_pr": {
    "enabled": true,
    "skip_branch_patterns": ["^\\d+\\.\\d+\\.\\d+$"],
    "base_from_fork_point": true,
    "base_branch_patterns": ["^\\d+\\.\\d+\\.\\d+$"]
  }
}
```

### auto_describe_pr — AI-written titles and descriptions

`auto_describe_pr` generates a PR's title and description from its **entire** diff (base...head, not just the latest push) with an AI model, whenever a PR is opened or marked ready for review, and again on every new commit.

| Field | Meaning |
|-------|---------|
| `enabled` | Turn the feature on (default `false`). |
| `model` | Optional model-name override; defaults to the model configured on the describe webhook. |

- The **description is rewritten** each time the diff changes. The existing description is given to the model, which carries forward any of its information that is still accurate — human-written notes survive.
- The **title is replaced only** when the existing one is a missing/autogenerated placeholder (branch-name junk, "update", ticket-only titles). A real title is left alone.
- Draft PRs are skipped until they're marked ready for review.
- A PR with **no net diff** (nothing for the model to describe — typically an auto-opened branch whose content already lives in the base) is titled `[zero diff] <branch>` instead of being skipped silently, so it never sits with its raw branch name as the title. Only pr-minder's own auto-opened PRs are *relabeled* this way — a human's title is never rewritten — and the marking is idempotent. (Closing an empty PR via `auto_open_pr.close_when_empty`, on by default, applies to *any* author's 0-diff PR — closing is reversible, a title rewrite is not.) A later non-empty commit re-describes normally.
- The work is deduped on a hash of the diff, so a commit that doesn't change the PR's effective diff (e.g. pr-minder's own branch-update merge) costs no model call. It can't loop: editing a PR's text isn't an event pr-minder reacts to.
- The slow model call doesn't run in the worker (real LLM calls outlive its time limits): the worker hands the diff to an internal webhook, fire-and-forget, which calls the model — retrying transient failures itself — and edits the PR. If newer commits arrive while a run is still in flight, the stale run is cancelled so descriptions can't land out of order.
- The webhook endpoint and its key are configured on the worker by whoever operates it; the model endpoint/key live with the webhook. Neither appears in repo config.
- **Failures are never silent — and never public.** If the feature is enabled but misconfigured (no webhook URL on the worker), or the hand-off fails (bad key, webhook down), pr-minder logs the error at error level for the operator (and the internal webhook runner's dashboard shows everything that reaches it: denied requests, failed runs). Nothing is ever posted on the PR; an enabled feature never silently does nothing.

```jsonc
{
  "auto_describe_pr": { "enabled": true }
}
```

## Org-wide config with per-repo overrides

In the `<org>/.github` repo at `.github/config/pr-minder/pr-minder.jsonc`:

```jsonc
{
  "$schema": "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/wow-look-at-my/pr-minder/master/schema/pr-minder.schema.json",
  "auto_update_pr": { "triggers": [{ "label": "automerge" }] },
  "auto_trigger_workflows": true,
  "repos": {
    "special-repo": {
      "auto_update_pr": { "triggers": [{ "label": "ready-to-merge" }] }
    },
    "opt-out-repo": {
      "auto_update_pr": { "triggers": [] }
    }
  }
}
```

In overrides, `auto_update_pr` replaces the parent's entirely; `auto_label_pr` and `auto_open_pr` merge field-by-field.

## Links

- Install: https://github.com/apps/pr-minder/installations/new
- Source code: https://github.com/wow-look-at-my/pr-minder
- Config JSON Schema: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/wow-look-at-my/pr-minder/master/schema/pr-minder.schema.json
